title: Eigenstate chaos in the presence of non-Abelian symmetries
abstract: The eigenstate thermalization hypothesis (ETH) posits that energy eigenstates encode local properties of the microcanonical ensemble. Motivated by recent interest in the physics of non-commuting conserved charges and the non-Abelian ETH, we study chaotic eigenstates in the presence of symmetries described by general compact Lie groups, such as SU(2). By applying non-Abelian symmetry resolution, we develop a non-Abelian microcanonical entropy and relate this entropy to the entanglement entropy of chaotic eigenstates. We find that microcanonical entropy is closely related to the symmetry-resolved entanglement entropy, which differs from conventional entanglement entropy by a universal logarithmic correction. Our results depend on the global Casimir charge, e.g. total spin. At finite charge density, we find a logarithmic enhancement to conventional entanglement entropy. At zero density, we find no such correction to entanglement entropy, but a logarithmic reduction to microcanonical entropy and symmetry-resolved entanglement entropy. We discuss the implications of our approach for non-Abelian eigenstate thermalization.
summary: This work develops a generalized framework for thermalization in quantum systems with non-Abelian symmetries, extending the ETH concept. By comparing non-Abelian microcanonical entropy to entanglement entropy, the authors derive universal logarithmic corrections that quantify the intrinsic entanglement associated with symmetry constraints. This advances our understanding of how local quantum information relates to global thermodynamic properties in complex many-body systems.
main_problem: The paper extends the Eigenstate Thermalization Hypothesis (ETH) to quantum many-body systems possessing non-Abelian symmetries, aiming to understand how thermalization occurs when multiple non-commuting conserved charges are present.
main_result: The non-Abelian microcanonical entropy is shown to be closely related to the symmetry-resolved entanglement entropy, with conventional entanglement entropy differing by a universal logarithmic correction dependent on the charge density regime.
method: The analysis employs non-Abelian symmetry resolution techniques, utilizing the replica trick and advanced techniques from representation theory (like the Wigner-Eckart theorem) to calculate entropy measures.
model_or_system: The study considers general quantum many-body systems with symmetries described by compact Lie groups (e.g., SU(2)), using the N-site next-neighbor Heisenberg chain as a prototypical model.
key_observables: Non-Abelian microcanonical entropy, symmetry-resolved entanglement entropy, conventional entanglement entropy, and the global Casimir charge.
important_parameters: Finite and zero charge density regimes, and the global Casimir charge (e.g., total spin).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the concept of simple reducibility and assumes the applicability of the Non-Abelian ETH ansatz.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the scaling of the squared Clebsch-Gordan coefficients and the relationship between different entropy measures in various polarization regimes.
paper_structure: The paper develops the theory by first establishing the non-Abelian ETH, then calculating the non-Abelian microcanonical entropy using replica methods, and finally comparing this result to entanglement entropy measures to quantify the logarithmic corrections.
why_interesting: It provides a rigorous theoretical framework for understanding thermalization in complex, interacting quantum systems where simple conservation laws are insufficient, directly impacting the understanding of quantum chaos and thermalization in strongly interacting regimes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

We diago- nalized a 14 site next-neighbor Heisenberg chain bipartitioned into 7 site subsys- tems with next neighbor interaction t = 0.5 and open boundary conditions without resolving discrete symmetries. Nonzero values of the reduced overlap were com- puted by explicitly resolving the SU(2) symmetry and dividing out the appropriate Clebsch–Gordan coefficients and found to agree with eq. (14).
